title: Dealing With a Coworker You Can't Confront
dek: You've been told that professional means silent, that maturity means swallowing your anger whole. But unspoken fury doesn't dissolve - it calcifies in your jaw, tightens your shoulders, and leaks out in ways you can't control. Here's how to metabolize what you can't say out loud.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. When you can't confront a coworker directly, your anger needs a witness and a container, not suppression. Strategic boundaries and private emotional practices protect your nervous system without requiring their cooperation. This approach honors both your integrity and the reality of workplace power dynamics.

You've been told that if you can't say it to their face, you shouldn't feel it at all. But anger doesn't work that way. It arrives whether or not you have permission, whether or not the org chart allows for honesty, whether or not your coworker has the capacity to hear what their behavior costs you. In my own practice, I've watched people torture themselves with this false binary: either confront or be complicit in your own erasure. There's a third way, and it starts with understanding that your anger is information, not ammunition. ## The Impossible Geometry of Workplace Honesty Your coworker undermines you in meetings. Or takes credit for your ideas. Or makes comments that land just shy of HR-actionable but leave you feeling stripped. And you can't confront them because they're your boss's favorite, or because you're in a probationary period, or because the last person who spoke up got quietly managed out six months later. The power differential is real. The risk is real. And your anger, unable to move toward its target, turns inward or sideways. You snap at your partner over dishes. You lose sleep rehearsing speeches you'll never give. Your body keeps score in tension headaches and a jaw that won't unclench. Thich Nhat Hanh wrote about anger as a crying baby that needs to be held, not abandoned. But at work, you can't exactly cradle your rage in the break room. The holding has to happen elsewhere, in containers you build deliberately. This isn't about "letting it go" - that tired phrase that means nothing. It's about giving your anger somewhere to live that isn't your body. ## What Your Body Already Knows Notice where you feel it. Right now, thinking about this coworker, where does the sensation land? For most people, it's the chest - a hot, pressurized feeling like carbonation in the sternum. Or the throat, thick and tight. Or the hands, which want to push or grip or make fists under the desk. Your nervous system is trying to complete an action that circumstances won't allow. The anger wants to move, to speak, to establish a boundary with force. When you trap it, your body doesn't forget the incomplete gesture. It loops, waiting for resolution that may never come through direct confrontation. So you give it a different kind of movement. In the bathroom stall between meetings, you press your palms hard against the cool tile wall and push. Actually push, with your full strength, for thirty seconds. This isn't metaphor. This is your body completing the blocked impulse to resist, to shove back against what's shoving you. The wall doesn't move, but your nervous system registers the action. Something unclenches. Or you write the email you'll never send. Every vicious, precise word. You don't save it in drafts - you write it in a paper journal or a text file that gets deleted. The point isn't the document. The point is letting your brain finish the sentence it keeps starting. ## Strategic Invisibility as Self-Preservation Beverly Engel, in her work on anger and boundaries, makes the case that withholding your emotional truth isn't always dishonesty - sometimes it's survival. There are relationships where full transparency would be feeding yourself to someone who can't or won't metabolize what you're offering. Your coworker might be one of those people. This doesn't make you fake. It makes you discerning. You can hold two truths: I am angry, and I am not safe to show it here. The anger doesn't need their validation to be real. It needs yours. So you practice strategic invisibility. You stop volunteering information about your projects until they're complete. You document everything in writing. You loop in witnesses to important conversations. These aren't passive-aggressive moves - they're architecture. You're building a structure that protects you from someone who has already shown you who they are. And you stop performing warmth you don't feel. Not rudeness, not coldness, but a neutral professionalism that doesn't cost you anything. "Thanks for the input" instead of "Great idea!" The smile that doesn't reach your eyes. Small, sustainable boundaries that don't require their permission. ## The Five-Minute Anger Practice You Can Do Anywhere When the feeling spikes - after a meeting where they interrupted you for the third time, after another email that makes your stomach drop - you need something immediate. Here's what works, and you can do it in a bathroom stall, your parked car, or behind a closed office door. Set a timer for five minutes. Close your eyes if you can. Put one hand on your chest, one on your belly. Breathe normally - don't force anything. Now, silently, give yourself permission to feel exactly what you feel. Not what you should feel, not what would be easier, but the actual texture of this moment. Name it: "I am furious." "I want to scream." "I hate that I have to manage this person's ego just to do my job." Let the thoughts come without editing. For three minutes, be the witness to your own experience. You're not solving anything. You're not planning your next move. You're just here, feeling what's true. For the last two minutes, imagine roots growing from the base of your spine down into the earth. Thick, strong roots that anchor you. This person can't shake you loose. Their behavior is about them. Your worth isn't up for vote. Breathe into that knowing until the timer goes off. This practice appears in various forms across the articles we've published on this site, and readers tell us it's the one they return to most. It doesn't fix the situation, but it keeps the anger from colonizing your entire nervous system. ## When Documentation Becomes Sacred Witness Keep a record. Not for HR, not for a lawsuit, though it might serve those purposes later. Keep it because your anger needs a witness, and if you can't tell anyone at work, the page becomes that witness. Date, time, what happened, what you felt. Specific quotes if you remember them. This isn't about building a case - though you might be, incidentally. It's about making your experience real outside your own head. When you're the only one who sees what's happening, you start to doubt your own perception. The journal says: this happened. You didn't imagine it. Your anger has cause. Over the years I've seen people discover that this simple practice - writing it down - shifts something fundamental. The anger becomes less wild, less likely to leak out in ways you don't intend. It has a place now. It's been seen. Some people keep this record in a locked note on their phone. Some use a paper journal they leave at home. The medium matters less than the consistency. You're training yourself to believe your own experience, even when no one else is backing you up. ## Building a Life Raft Outside Work Your coworker takes up space in your head that they haven't earned. They're there at dinner, in the shower, in the space between sleep and waking. This is the real cost - not just the hours at work, but the way they follow you home. You need a counterweight. Something in your life that has nothing to do with this person, this job, this situation. Thomas Moore writes about the soul's need for beauty and ritual, especially when we're trapped in circumstances that grind us down. He's not talking about bubble baths and scented candles - though there's nothing wrong with those. He means something you can devote yourself to that reminds you who you are outside this dynamic. Maybe it's a Tuesday night pottery class where your hands are in clay and your mind goes quiet. Maybe it's the morning pages you write before work, three pages of stream-of-consciousness that clear the channel. Maybe it's the weekly call with a friend who knew you before this job and will know you after. In our experience, people who survive toxic work situations intact are the ones who built something beautiful elsewhere. This isn't distraction. It's ballast. When you have a life that nourishes you, the coworker becomes smaller. Still present, still difficult, but not the entire story. You can reference [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for nervous system support during this kind of sustained stress - adaptogens like holy basil and ashwagandha can help your body handle the chronic activation. Some people find that [Yogi Soothing Caramel Bedtime Tea (64 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B083STP4NN?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps them sleep when work anxiety follows them home. ## What Anger Teaches When You Can't Use It Directly Here's the paradox: the anger you can't express to your coworker might be teaching you more than a successful confrontation ever would. It's showing you where your boundaries are, what you value, what you will and won't tolerate in the next job. It's information about who you want to become. The APA's research summary on anger management confirms what many of us have learned through hard experience: suppressing anger correlates with health problems, but so does expressing it without skill or safety. The middle path involves acknowledging the feeling, understanding its message, and choosing your response based on what will actually serve you. Sometimes what serves you is leaving. Not immediately - you need time to plan, to save, to line up the next thing. But the anger becomes fuel for your exit strategy instead of poison you swallow daily. You start updating your resume. You reach out to your network. You remember that this job is not your identity, and this coworker is not your keeper. And sometimes what serves you is staying, but with radical acceptance that this person will not change, and your peace cannot depend on their transformation. You lower your expectations to the floor and stop hoping for different. This isn't defeat. It's strategy. You do your work, collect your paycheck, and invest your real energy elsewhere. Beverly Engel's [Honor Your Anger - Beverly Engel](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0806540788?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a framework for this kind of discernment - when to speak, when to act, when to simply bear witness to your own experience and choose preservation over confrontation. ## The Body's Long Memory Your shoulders remember every time you didn't say what you meant. Your jaw remembers every smile you manufactured. The body keeps its own accounts, and if you're not careful, you'll cash out with chronic pain, digestive issues, or the kind of exhaustion that sleep doesn't touch. This is why the anger needs tending. Not resolution, necessarily, but tending. You might work with a massage therapist who understands trauma and can help release what's stored in your tissues. You might try somatic therapy, where the focus is less on talking about the coworker and more on tracking what happens in your body when you think about them. One woman I know describes it this way: every time her coworker's name appeared in her inbox, her ribcage would contract, like her body was trying to make itself smaller. She started pausing before opening those emails to take three full breaths and consciously expand her chest. A tiny practice, but over weeks it retrained her nervous system. The coworker's power over her physiology diminished. This is the work when you can't confront: you confront what's happening inside you instead. You meet the anger, the fear, the sense of helplessness. You give your body new information - you're safe right now, you have choices, this situation is temporary. The more you tend to your own system, the less the coworker can destabilize you. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Isn't this just letting them win? No. Letting them win would be internalizing their narrative, believing their implied assessment of you, shrinking your work to avoid their attention. What we're describing is protecting your energy and integrity while you figure out your next move. You're not conceding - you're refusing to play a game that's rigged. Sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is not give someone the fight they're trying to pick. ### How do I know if I'm being too sensitive or if this is actually wrong? If you're asking this question, you've probably been told you're too sensitive before. Here's the test: does this behavior violate norms that would apply to anyone, or is it personal to you? If they interrupt everyone, that's their problem. If they only interrupt you and people who share your identity, that's discrimination. But even if it's "just" their problem, your anger at being interrupted is still valid. You don't need an objective panel of judges to grant your feelings legitimacy. If it hurts, it hurts. ### What if staying in this job is making me physically sick? Then you have your answer, even if you don't want it. [When the Body Says No - Gabor Maté](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1583949933?tag=spankyspinola-20) documents the clear links between unexpressed emotion and illness. If you're developing stress-related symptoms - migraines, IBS, chronic infections, blood pressure issues - your body is telling you the cost is too high. Start making your exit plan, even if it takes a year. In the meantime, get medical support, lean on [more articles in this journal](/articles) for practices that can help, and be honest with yourself about the timeline. Some jobs are worth staying in while you prepare to leave. Some are actively dangerous to your health. ## The Long View and the Daily Practice Years from now, you might barely remember this coworker's name. You'll remember how you handled yourself, whether you abandoned yourself to keep the peace, whether you found ways to honor your anger without letting it consume you. This is the real work - not changing them, not getting them to see what they're doing, but staying awake to your own experience and choosing responses that let you sleep at night. Some days that looks like strategic silence. Some days it looks like a carefully worded email that documents their behavior without accusation. Some days it looks like crying in your car before you drive home, then washing your face and walking into your real life. The anger doesn't make you unprofessional. The anger makes you human, and it's pointing you toward something that matters - your own dignity, your need for respect, your right to work without this particular form of suffering. Listen to it. Let it teach you. And when you're ready, let it move you toward something better, even if that movement is slow and requires more patience than you wish you had. You can find more on building sustainable anger practices at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and if you need a place to start, visit [the about page](/about) to understand the philosophy behind this work. Your anger is not your enemy. Your coworker is not your teacher, except accidentally. And you are not required to light yourself on fire to prove you're warm-blooded. The practice is staying lit on your own terms, in conditions that want to extinguish you. That's enough. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on dealing with a coworker you can't confront, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
